mappings.rationaleAI: Scientific reasoning, the theory-evidence relationship, and the demarcation problem directly address the philosophy of science (B). Coverage of scientific explanation and paradigm change connects to elements of the scientific enterprise (B.3), movements of scientific thought (B.4), and the philosophical status of scientific theory (B.5).

Sixteenth and seventeenth century developments including Vesalius, Galileo, Harvey, Descartes, Boyle, and Newton directly address European science in the early modern period (A.4), the rebirth of science in the Renaissance (A.4.a), and the revolution in natural philosophy (A.4.b).
Scientific ideas in ancient Greece and Rome from the 8th century BCE to the 6th century CE directly address science in ancient Western civilization (A.2) and science in Greek civilization (A.2.a).

Galileo's contributions to the scientific revolution and his break from Aristotelian physics directly address the revolution in natural philosophy (A.4.b) and the radical reformulation of natural knowledge (A.4.b.i). His development of scientific method connects to the 17th-century debate about methodology (B.2.b).

Newton's contributions to physics, mathematics, and astronomy relate to the revolution in natural philosophy (A.4.b) and results of the new philosophy (A.4.b.ii). His work exemplifies Empiricist and Rationalist approaches in the 18th century (B.2.c).
The heliocentric revolution during the Renaissance directly addresses the rebirth of science in the Renaissance (A.4.a) and the revolution in natural philosophy (A.4.b).

Descartes's mathematical approach to natural science and methodological doubt directly address the radical reformulation of natural knowledge (A.4.b.i) and the debate about scientific methodology (B.2.b).
Aristotle's scientific researches directly address the development of scientific attitudes in Greek civilization (A.2.a.ii) and the alternative viewpoints of Platonists and Aristotelians (B.2.a).
Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection, published in 1859, directly addresses science in the 19th century (A.6).

Physics spanning 2,500 years covers the full history of science (A), including ancient (A.2), Islamic (A.3), early modern (A.4), Industrial Revolution (A.5), 19th century (A.6), and 20th century (A.7).
Historical development of chemistry from Boyle through Lavoisier to the periodic table covers the early modern period (A.4), the Industrial Revolution era (A.5), and 19th-century science (A.6).
Medicine from Hippocratic traditions through modern biomedicine addresses ancient science (A.2), early modern science (A.4), 19th-century science (A.6), and 20th-century science (A.7).
Mathematical history across cultures including the medieval Islamic world relates to science in other civilizations (A.3).
The Galileo affair, the Scopes trial, and theological implications of science address contemporary problems facing science (A.8) and the relevance of scientific knowledge to other spheres (B.6).
The demarcation problem relates to the nature and scope of philosophy of science (B.1), formal structures of science (B.3.c), and philosophical status of scientific theory (B.5).
Philosophical foundations of modern physics address philosophy of science broadly (B), the 20th-century debate (B.2.e), and philosophical status of scientific theory (B.5).
Philosophical analysis of Darwinian evolution relates to the application of different philosophical approaches to different sciences (B.5.b).
Faraday's scientific contributions in nineteenth-century Britain directly address 19th-century science (A.6).
Bohr's contributions to atomic structure and quantum mechanics relate to science in the early 20th century (A.7).
Hume's problem of induction relates to Empiricist interpretations of Newtonian physics (B.2.c) and the role of intuition in scientific discovery (B.4.a).
Kant's synthesis of rationalism and empiricism relates to Kantian interpretations of Newtonian physics (B.2.c) and the influence of Kant's belief in unique rationality (B.2.d).
The Enlightenment's achievements relate to the intellectual origins of revolution and the spirit of the Enlightenment (A.5.b).
Analysis of objectivity in science relates to the relative importance of observation, theory, and formulation (B.3.a) and the epistemological status of scientific propositions (B.5.a).
Philosophical analysis of causation's role in scientific explanation relates to elements of the scientific enterprise (B.3) and validation of scientific theories (B.4.b).
Theory and practice of measurement directly addresses empirical procedures of science including measurement (B.3.b).
History of astronomy from Babylonian methods through the Copernican revolution addresses ancient science (A.2.a), early modern science (A.4), and 19th-century developments (A.6).
Einstein's theories of relativity relate to 20th-century science (A.7) and the 20th-century debate responding to relativity (B.2.e).
Quantum theory relates to 20th-century science (A.7) and the philosophical debate responding to quantum mechanics (B.2.e).
Pavlov's Nobel Prize-winning research relates to science in the early 20th century (A.7).
The history of eugenics relates to contemporary moral difficulties facing science (A.8) and the social significance of science (B.6).
History of time measurement relates to science in ancient civilizations (A.2), and calendar development connects to empirical procedures of science (B.3.b).
Engineering connects to science in the early modern period (A.4) and science during the Industrial Revolution (A.5).
Plato's theory of Forms relates to the Platonic view of nature in Greek civilization (A.2.a.ii).
Phenomenology, existentialism, and the relationship between philosophy and science connect to the 20th-century debate (B.2.e).
Montaigne as an early modern skeptic relates to the state of science in the early Renaissance (A.4.a).
